items.title: Deterministic templates write all committed code; the LLM only proposes
items.description: The generator must emit every line of committed source from deterministic templates and a module-spec JSON. A local LLM may only PROPOSE a catalogue (suggested entities, endpoints, services, navigation); it never authors a committed file directly. The proposal is data the deterministic stage consumes, not code it ships.
items.applicability: The core split for any LocalAIFactory generator run that optionally consults a local model.
items.example: qwen2.5-coder proposes a 'PurchaseReturn' entity; the deterministic template, not the model, renders PurchaseReturn.cs, its DbSet, controller and view from that one catalogue row.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: keep a hard boundary so model text is parsed into a validated catalogue object, never spliced into a .cs file. Test implication: assert a run with the model disabled produces byte-stable deterministic output and a run with a hostile proposal still emits only template code. Generator implication: the proposal feeds injection markers only. Boundary: this guarantees reproducibility of the deterministic surface, not correctness of the model's suggestions.

items.title: Keep the generator dependency-free so it runs from a fresh clone
items.description: The generator (tools/LocalAIFactory.Generator) must have no runtime dependency on external services, NuGet plugins, or a populated database to emit a solution. It reads templates and a spec from disk and writes files; any optional model call degrades to skipped.
items.applicability: Every invocation, especially CI and fresh-clone reproduction checks.
items.example: On a machine with no Ollama and no internet, the generator still emits the full ERP solution; only the LLM catalogue proposal is recorded as 'unavailable'.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: inject any optional client behind an interface that returns 'unavailable' rather than throwing. Test implication: a clean checkout with no services emits a buildable solution. Generator implication: the dependency-free core is the contract; optional enrichment sits outside it. Boundary: dependency-free emission does not by itself prove the emitted product builds — that needs a separate build gate.

items.title: Drive the whole solution from a single module-spec JSON
items.description: A module-spec JSON is the single source of truth for which modules, entities, fields and relationships the generator emits. The templates are mechanical; intent lives in the spec. Changing what is generated means editing the spec, not the output.
items.applicability: Adding or reshaping a generated module (accounting, stock, selling, buying, manufacturing).
items.example: Adding a 'CostCenter' dimension is one spec edit that fans out to entity, DbSet, migration model, CRUD UI and report scope on the next emit.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: validate the spec against a schema before emission so a malformed field fails fast. Test implication: a known spec produces a known file manifest. Generator implication: the spec is the contract surface humans edit. Boundary: the spec governs structure, not business-rule correctness, which still requires hand-authored domain templates and tests.

items.title: Validate every LLM catalogue proposal before it touches a template
items.description: A local-LLM catalogue proposal is untrusted input. Before use it must pass validation: well-formed schema, known field types, references resolve, and the proposed names map to legal identifiers. Anything that fails validation is dropped with a logged reason, not silently coerced.
items.applicability: The boundary between the model's suggestion and the deterministic emission stage.
items.example: A proposed entity with a field typed 'currency-ish' fails type validation and is rejected, while a clean proposal of an 'Account' entity with decimal fields is accepted into the catalogue.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: parse-then-validate, never trust-then-fix. Test implication: feed malformed, partial and over-large proposals and assert each is rejected with a recorded reason. Generator implication: only validated catalogue rows reach the injection markers. Boundary: validation enforces structural legality, not semantic usefulness of the suggestion.

items.title: Guard catalogue proposals against name collisions
items.description: Before a proposed entity, endpoint or service enters the catalogue, the generator must check it does not collide with an existing deterministic name or another accepted proposal. Collisions are rejected rather than overwriting or shadowing existing emitted code.
items.applicability: Merging an LLM proposal into a catalogue that already contains deterministic baseline items.
items.example: The model proposes a 'PaymentEntry' service but the deterministic baseline already defines one; the proposal is rejected as a collision and the baseline is kept.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: maintain a reserved set of baseline names and a running set of accepted proposals, and check both. Test implication: a proposal duplicating a baseline name is rejected; a proposal duplicating an earlier proposal is rejected. Generator implication: collision-free catalogue is a precondition for injection. Boundary: collision detection is by identifier, so semantically duplicated concepts under different names are not caught here.

items.title: Guard catalogue proposals against reserved and language-keyword names
items.description: Proposed names must be screened against C# keywords, framework reserved identifiers, and project-reserved names. A proposal whose name would produce an illegal or shadowing identifier is rejected before emission so the generated solution always compiles.
items.applicability: Identifier generation for entities, DbSets, controllers and routes from proposals.
items.example: A proposed entity named 'class' or 'Internal' is rejected; the generator never emits 'public class class { }'.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: keep an explicit deny-list plus an identifier-legality check, applied before any file is written. Test implication: proposals using keywords, leading digits, and reserved framework names are each rejected. Generator implication: only legal identifiers reach templates. Boundary: a legal identifier can still be a poor name; this guard prevents compile failures, not bad taste.

items.title: Guard against hallucinated references in a proposal
items.description: A local model can invent fields, relationships or types that do not exist. The generator must resolve every reference in a proposal against the known catalogue and reject any item that points at a non-existent entity, field or type rather than emitting a dangling reference.
items.applicability: Accepting proposed relationships and foreign keys from a model.
items.example: The model proposes a relationship to entity 'Ledger' that is nowhere defined; the relationship is dropped and the failure is logged as a hallucinated reference.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: resolve references in a single pass after all entities are known, then prune unresolved links. Test implication: a proposal referencing an undefined entity is rejected and the rest of the proposal still processes. Generator implication: only fully-resolvable catalogue rows are injected. Boundary: reference resolution catches dangling links, not plausible-but-wrong domain modelling.

items.title: Wire catalogue items into templates through explicit injection markers
items.description: Templates carry explicit injection markers — __CATALOG_DBSETS__, __CATALOG_ENDPOINTS__, __CATALOG_SERVICES__, __CATALOG_NAV__ — that the generator replaces with rendered catalogue content. Generated extension points are confined to these markers so the surrounding template stays deterministic and reviewable.
items.applicability: Composing deterministic baseline code with validated catalogue additions.
items.example: Accepted DbSet rows render into the __CATALOG_DBSETS__ marker inside the context template; everything outside the marker is unchanged baseline code.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: markers must be uniquely named and idempotent so re-emission replaces, not appends. Test implication: emitting twice yields identical output and markers never leak into the final file. Generator implication: only marker regions vary between runs with and without proposals. Boundary: markers localise variability; they do not validate that injected content is semantically coherent with the baseline.

items.title: Write every manual fix back into templates, specs or Program.cs, never the product
items.description: When a generated product needs a fix, the fix is made in the template, the module-spec, or the generator's Program.cs and then re-emitted. A one-off edit to the generated output is forbidden because the next emission silently reverts it and reproduction drifts.
items.applicability: Any defect found in generated code during a sprint.
items.example: A missing null-check in a generated controller is fixed in the controller template, then the whole module is re-emitted so every generated controller carries the fix.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: treat generated directories as build output, not editable source. Test implication: re-emitting after a template fix reproduces the fix in every affected file. Generator implication: the template/spec is the only writable surface. Boundary: this discipline keeps reproduction honest but slows quick experiments, which must still round-trip through the template.

items.title: Measure reproduction as test%, module% and deterministic-surface%
items.description: Reproduction fidelity is reported as three honest numbers: percentage of tests reproduced, percentage of modules reproduced, and percentage of the deterministic surface reproduced. A single headline figure hides where the gap is, so all three are kept and the gap is explained.
items.applicability: Reporting how faithfully a fresh emission matches the curated reference solution.
items.example: A Gold-generated run reports ~92% of tests, ~100% of the deterministic surface, with the shortfall attributed to non-deterministic LLM modules.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: compute each percentage from a manifest diff, not from a vibe. Test implication: the reproduction report is itself asserted against expected thresholds. Generator implication: the three numbers guide where to invest in templating. Boundary: percentages measure structural reproduction, not runtime behaviour parity, which needs the test suite to pass on the emitted product.

items.title: The reproduction gap equals the non-deterministic LLM modules and their tests
items.description: When the deterministic surface reproduces at 100% but tests reproduce below 100%, the difference is exactly the LLM-authored modules and the tests that exercise them. Naming this gap precisely prevents inflating or hiding it and points at the only way to close it: templatise the module.
items.applicability: Explaining why a Gold-generated run is ~92% on tests rather than 100%.
items.example: Eight non-deterministic modules and their tests account for the entire 8-point test shortfall; nothing else is missing.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: attribute every non-reproduced test to a specific module so the gap is auditable. Test implication: the attribution itself is verified by a diff that lists each non-reproduced test against its module. Generator implication: closing the gap means promoting a module from LLM-authored to template-authored. Boundary: some modules may be intentionally left non-deterministic, so a 100% target is a choice, not a requirement.

items.title: Set EnableDefaultItems=false and Compile templates explicitly to avoid double-compilation
items.description: When the generator's own project sits near template .cs files, default item globbing can pull templates into the generator build and compile them twice. Set EnableDefaultItems=false and declare explicit Compile items so template sources are treated as content, not code the generator compiles.
items.applicability: Project layout where template .cs files live inside or beside a buildable generator project.
items.example: Without the guard, AccountTemplate.cs is compiled both as a template payload and as generator source, producing duplicate-type errors; explicit Compile items fix it.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: mark template .cs as None/Content and list real generator sources explicitly. Test implication: the generator project builds clean with zero duplicate-type errors. Generator implication: templates ship as text payloads, not compiled assemblies. Boundary: explicit item lists must be maintained when files are added, trading a little upkeep for build correctness.

items.title: Target .NET 10 with .slnx, not the legacy .sln format
items.description: On .NET 10 the generator emits and the tooling expects the XML solution format .slnx rather than the legacy .sln. Generated solution scaffolding, scripts and build commands must reference .slnx consistently so the emitted product opens and builds with current tooling.
items.applicability: Emitting and building generated multi-project solutions under .NET 10.
items.example: The generator writes LocalAIFactory.slnx and the build script runs 'dotnet build LocalAIFactory.slnx' instead of the old .sln path.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: template the solution as .slnx and update any helper script that hard-codes .sln. Test implication: a build invoked against the emitted .slnx succeeds. Generator implication: solution format is part of the deterministic surface. Boundary: older tooling that only reads .sln will not open the emitted solution, which is an accepted .NET 10 constraint.

items.title: Emit attribution and knowledge-usage JSON alongside the product
items.description: Each generation run emits machine-readable provenance: an attribution record of which templates, spec version and (if any) model proposal contributed, plus a knowledge-usage JSON listing which knowledge items informed the emission. This makes a generated product auditable after the fact.
items.applicability: Any run that must be explainable or reproduced later.
items.example: The run writes attribution.json naming the spec hash and template set, and knowledge-usage.json listing the rules applied, so a reviewer can trace any file to its origin.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: write provenance as part of the emit step, not as an afterthought. Test implication: the provenance files exist, are valid JSON, and reference the actual inputs used. Generator implication: provenance is a first-class output. Boundary: provenance records what was used, not whether the result is correct.

items.title: Prove self-sufficiency by regenerating from a fresh clone each sprint
items.description: Each sprint runs the generator from a clean checkout into an empty output directory and confirms it emits a buildable, testable solution with no manual touch-ups. This fresh-clone regeneration is the proof that templates and specs, not accumulated local state, hold the product.
items.applicability: Sprint exit criteria for the generator.
items.example: A CI job clones the repo, runs the generator, builds the emitted .slnx and runs its tests; green means the generator is self-sufficient this sprint.
items.limitation: Implementation implication: the regeneration job must start from nothing, not from a warm working tree. Test implication: emitted solution builds 0-error and its deterministic tests pass from the fresh clone. Generator implication: self-sufficiency is re-proven, not assumed. Boundary: fresh-clone proof covers what the generator emits deterministically; non-deterministic LLM modules are validated separately.
